While Moses was in the mountain, receiving the law from God, the people made a loud appeal to Aaron. This excited crowd was tired of waiting for Moses’ return. Weariness in waiting leads many to temptation. The Lord must be waited for until he comes, and waited for even though he delays. Let their eagerness to part with their earrings to make an idol shame our stinginess in the service of the true God. They did not hesitate because of the cost of their idolatry; and should we begrudge the expenses of religion? Aaron made the shape of an ox or calf, giving it some finish with a graving tool. They offered sacrifice to this idol. Having set up an image before them, and so changed the truth of God into a lie, their sacrifices were an abomination. Had they not, only a few days before, in this very place, heard the voice of the Lord God speaking to them out of the midst of the fire, You shall not make to yourself any graven image? Had they not themselves solemnly entered into covenant with God, that they would do all he had said to them, and would be obedient? Yet before they moved from the place where this covenant had been solemnly made, they broke an express command, in defiance of an express warning. It plainly shows that the law was no more able to make holy than it was to justify; by it is the knowledge of sin, but not the cure of sin. Aaron was set apart by divine appointment to the office of the priesthood; but he, who had once shamed himself so far as to build an altar to a golden calf, must acknowledge himself unworthy of the honor of attending at the altar of God, and indebted to free grace alone for it. Thus pride and boasting were silenced.

God says to Moses that the Israelites had corrupted themselves. Sin is the corruption of the sinner, and it is a self-corruption; every person is tempted when they are drawn aside by their own desire. They turned aside out of the way. Sin is a departure from the way of duty into a side path. They soon forgot God’s works. He sees what they cannot discover, nor is any wickedness of the world hidden from him. We could not bear to see even one thousandth part of that evil which God sees every day. God expresses the greatness of his just displeasure, in the manner of people who would have Moses' prayer save them from ruin; thus he was a type of Christ, by whose mediation alone God will reconcile the world to himself. Moses pleads God’s glory. Glorifying God’s name, as it ought to be our first petition, is so in the Lord’s prayer, and so it ought to be our great plea. And God's promises are to be our pleas in prayer; for what he has promised he is able to perform. See the power of prayer. In answer to Moses’ prayers, God showed his purpose of sparing the people, as he had before seemed determined on their destruction; which change in the outward discovery of his purpose is called repenting of the evil.

What a change it is to come down from the mountain of communion with God to converse with a wicked world. In God we see nothing but what is pure and pleasing; in the world nothing but what is sinful and provoking. To show that an idol is nothing, Moses ground the calf to dust. Mixing this powder with their drink signified that someone who turns away from God will be filled with their own desires.

No wise person has ever made a more trifling and foolish excuse than Aaron’s. We must never be drawn into sin by anything someone says or does to us, because people can only tempt us; they cannot force us. Moses' approach turned the dancing into trembling. They were exposed to shame because of their sin. The way Moses dealt with this reproach was not by concealing the sin or putting a false light on it, but by punishing it. The Levites were to kill the leaders in this wickedness, yet only those who openly stepped forward were executed. Those who persist in sin are marked for ruin; those who were shouting and dancing in the morning were dying before night. The judgments of the Lord sometimes bring such sudden changes to sinners who are secure and joyful in their sin.

Moses calls it a great sin. The work of ministers is to show people the greatness of their sins. The great evil of sin appears in the price of pardon. Moses pleads with God for mercy; he came not to make excuses, but to make atonement. We are not to suppose that Moses means that he would be willing to perish forever for the people's sake. We are to love our neighbor as ourselves, and not more than ourselves. But having the mind which was in Christ, he was willing to lay down his life in the most painful manner if he might thereby preserve the people. Moses could not wholly turn away God’s wrath, which shows that the law of Moses was not able to reconcile men to God and to perfect our peace with him. In Christ alone, God so pardons sin as to remember it no more. From this history we see that no unhumbled, carnal heart can long endure the holy precepts, the humbling truths, and the spiritual worship of God. But a god, a priest, a worship, a doctrine, and a sacrifice suited to the carnal mind will ever meet with abundance of worshippers. The very gospel itself may be so perverted as to suit a worldly taste. Well is it for us that the Prophet like unto Moses, but who is beyond compare more powerful and merciful, has made atonement for our souls and now intercedes in our behalf. Let us rejoice in his grace.
